title: Laptop hardware components
objectives: Identify and replace laptop hardware components

sections.body: A laptop packs the same functions as a desktop into a portable chassis, so its parts are smaller, more integrated, and often proprietary. Knowing which components are field-replaceable — and which are soldered down — tells you whether a fault is a quick fix or a board-level repair.

sections.heading: Memory and storage
sections.body: Laptops use compact form factors that differ from their desktop equivalents:
bullets: RAM uses `SO-DIMM` modules, which are physically shorter than desktop DIMMs.
Storage is commonly an `M.2` SSD (NVMe over PCIe, or slower SATA), or a 2.5-inch SATA SSD in older units.
Some thin laptops solder RAM and storage to the board, making them non-upgradeable — always check before quoting an upgrade.

sections.heading: Commonly replaced parts
sections.body: The parts you will swap most often are the battery, keyboard, RAM, storage, and the wireless card (an `M.2` Key A/E module). The CPU and GPU are usually surface-mounted and not serviceable in the field.

sections.heading: Inside the lid
sections.body: The display assembly carries more than the panel: it also houses the webcam, microphone, and the Wi-Fi/Bluetooth antennas, whose cables route down through the hinge. A damaged hinge can therefore cause flaky wireless or a dead camera, not just a loose screen.

The display is a stack of parts working together: a panel that produces the image, a backlight that illuminates it, and — on touchscreens — a digitizer that turns finger contact into coordinates. Matching a symptom to the right layer is the key skill.

image.alt: Diagram of laptop display layers: digitizer on top, then the LCD or OLED panel, then the backlight.
image.caption: The display is a stack — digitizer, panel, then backlight.

sections.heading: Panel types
sections.body: Three technologies dominate, each with trade-offs:
bullets: `TN` — cheap and fast, but poor viewing angles and color.
`IPS` — accurate color and wide viewing angles, the modern default.
`OLED` — per-pixel lighting for deep blacks and contrast, but risks burn-in over time.

sections.heading: Backlight faults
sections.body: Most laptops use an LED backlight; older ones used a CCFL tube driven by an inverter. The classic symptom: the screen is very dim but a faint image is visible under a flashlight. That means the panel is still drawing pixels, so the fault is the backlight or its power circuit — not the panel itself.

sections.heading: Digitizer issues
sections.body: The digitizer maps physical touch to screen coordinates. If taps register a noticeable distance from where the user presses, the digitizer needs recalibration or replacement — the LCD panel is fine because it is still displaying correctly.

Mobile devices juggle several wireless radios at once, each suited to a different range and purpose. Knowing which radio handles which job lets you pick the right setting to toggle when something will not connect.

sections.heading: The wireless radios
sections.body: From shortest range to longest:
bullets: `NFC` — a few centimeters, used for tap-to-pay and quick pairing.
`Bluetooth` — a personal-area network for peripherals like headsets and keyboards.
`Wi-Fi` — local network and internet access in homes and offices.
`Cellular` (LTE/5G) — wide-area data when away from Wi-Fi.

sections.heading: Pairing Bluetooth
sections.body: The pairing flow is consistent across devices: enable Bluetooth, make the accessory discoverable, select it from the list on the host, confirm any code, then test. If a device will not appear, it is usually not in discoverable mode or is already paired to something else.

sections.heading: Sharing a connection
sections.body: A phone can share its cellular data as a hotspot (over Wi-Fi) or by tethering (over USB or Bluetooth). Airplane mode disables all radios at once; you can then re-enable Wi-Fi or Bluetooth individually if the venue allows it.

Modern laptops lean on a small set of versatile ports plus accessories that expand them. Understanding what each port can carry — and how USB-C consolidates several roles — saves you from recommending the wrong adapter or dock.

sections.heading: Ports you will meet
sections.body: Common connectors and what they carry:
bullets: `USB-C` — data, video (DisplayPort Alt Mode), and power delivery over one reversible connector.
`Thunderbolt` — high-speed data and video, physically using the USB-C connector.
`HDMI` / `DisplayPort` — external displays.
`USB-A` — legacy peripherals and flash drives.

sections.heading: Docks vs. port replicators
sections.body: A port replicator simply breaks one connection out into more of the same ports. A docking station does more — adding network, extra displays, and charging — and often connects over a single Thunderbolt or USB-C cable, turning a laptop into a desktop workstation.

sections.heading: Power and battery life
sections.body: USB-C Power Delivery (`USB-C PD`) lets one cable both charge the laptop and carry data. To extend runtime away from an outlet, enable a battery-saver power plan, which lowers CPU and display power draw — a more effective first step than replacing the battery.

A port number is the address of a service on a device. When a packet arrives, the port tells the system which application should handle it. Memorizing the common ports — and whether each rides on TCP, UDP, or both — is one of the highest-value things you can do for the exam and the job.

image.alt: The four layers of the TCP/IP model from Application down to Link, with example protocols at each layer.
image.caption: Ports live at the Transport layer of the TCP/IP model.

sections.heading: Ports worth memorizing
sections.body: The frequently tested set:
bullets: `20`/`21` FTP, `22` SSH/SFTP, `23` Telnet, `25` SMTP

sections.heading: TCP vs. UDP
sections.body: TCP is connection-oriented: it sets up a session and retransmits lost data, so it suits web, email, and file transfer. UDP is connectionless and fast with no delivery guarantee, which is why it carries DNS lookups, DHCP, and real-time voice and video where speed beats perfect accuracy.

sections.heading: Secure vs. insecure pairs
sections.body: Many protocols come in a plaintext version and an encrypted replacement. Prefer the secure one: `HTTPS` over HTTP, `SSH` over Telnet, and `SFTP`/`FTPS` over plain FTP. Knowing the secure alternative is a recurring exam theme.
Wi-Fi has evolved through several 802.11 standards, each faster than the last. The other half of the picture is frequency: the band a network uses trades range against speed and interference.

sections.heading: The 802.11 generations
sections.body: Newest first, with their marketing names:
bullets: `802.11ax` (Wi-Fi 6) — highest throughput, efficient in crowded areas, 2.4 and 5 GHz (6 GHz on 6E).
`802.11ac` (Wi-Fi 5) — 5 GHz only, fast and widely deployed.
`802.11n` (Wi-Fi 4) — dual-band, the first to use MIMO.
`802.11g`/`802.11b` — older 2.4 GHz standards.

sections.heading: 2.4 GHz vs. 5 GHz
sections.body: The `2.4 GHz` band travels farther and through walls better but is slower and crowded with non-Wi-Fi devices. The `5 GHz` band is faster with more channels and less interference, but its range is shorter. Choose based on whether the user needs coverage or speed.

sections.heading: Securing the wireless
sections.body: Use the strongest encryption the hardware supports — `WPA3` if available, otherwise `WPA2` with AES. Avoid the deprecated WEP and original WPA, which are trivially broken.

title: Configuring a SOHO network
objectives: Configure a small office / home office network

sections.body: A small office / home office (SOHO) router combines several roles in one box: it routes between your LAN and the internet, hands out addresses, translates private addresses, and serves as a wireless access point. Configuring one safely is a core technician task.

sections.heading: Addressing with DHCP and NAT
sections.body: DHCP automatically leases each device an IP address, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS server — the DORA sequence (Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge). NAT (Network Address Translation) lets many private devices share one public IP address provided by the ISP.

sections.heading: Private address ranges
sections.body: Internal networks use reserved private ranges that are not routable on the internet:

sections.heading: First hardening steps
sections.body: Before a router goes live, change the default admin password, update its firmware, set strong WPA2/WPA3 wireless encryption, and disable remote management and WPS. These few steps remove the most common ways SOHO networks get compromised.

title: Cables, connectors, and tools
objectives: Select and use networking tools and media

sections.body: Physical media still underpins most networks. Knowing which cable to run and which tool diagnoses which fault keeps you from guessing when a connection is flaky.

sections.heading: Copper and fiber
sections.body: Common media and their use:
bullets: `Cat 5e`/`Cat 6` twisted pair — standard Ethernet up to 1–10 Gbps over short runs.
Fiber (single-mode or multimode) — long distance and high speed, immune to electrical interference.
Coaxial — cable internet and some CCTV.

sections.heading: Termination tools
sections.body: A `crimper` attaches RJ45 plugs to patch cables; a `punchdown tool` seats wires into a wall jack or patch panel. A cable tester then confirms each pin is wired straight through with no opens or shorts.

sections.heading: Diagnostic tools
sections.body: A `toner probe` traces which cable in a bundle is which. A `loopback plug` tests a port by feeding its output back to its input. For software-side checks, `ping` tests reachability and `ipconfig` shows a host's address settings.

title: Motherboards, CPUs, and expansion
objectives: Identify motherboard form factors and components

sections.body: The motherboard is the backbone that ties every component together. Its form factor sets its size and how many slots it has, while the CPU socket and expansion slots determine what you can install.

sections.heading: Form factors and slots
sections.body: From largest to smallest: `ATX`, `microATX`, and `Mini-ITX`. Larger boards offer more expansion (RAM and PCIe slots); smaller ones suit compact builds. Expansion cards use `PCIe` slots, sized by lane count such as x1 and x16 (x16 for graphics).

sections.heading: CPU sockets
sections.body: A CPU is matched to a specific socket. Intel uses LGA sockets where the pins are on the board; AMD's modern AM4/AM5 are also pin-on-board. You cannot mix a CPU and socket that do not match, so always confirm compatibility before ordering.

sections.heading: Firmware and the CMOS battery
sections.body: The UEFI/BIOS firmware initializes hardware at startup and stores settings like boot order. A small `CMOS battery` keeps those settings and the clock alive when the system is unplugged — a dead one causes the time to reset and boot settings to be lost.
Memory holds the data a system is actively working on; storage keeps it permanently. Picking the right type — and recognizing the connector — is everyday hardware work.

sections.heading: RAM types
sections.body: Desktops use full-size `DIMM` modules; laptops use shorter `SO-DIMM`s. Generations are not interchangeable — `DDR4` and `DDR5` are keyed differently and will not fit the wrong slot. Some systems support dual-channel, which pairs modules for more bandwidth.

sections.heading: Storage technologies
sections.body: Speed and connector, slowest to fastest:
bullets: Hard disk drive (`HDD`) — spinning platters, cheap capacity, slowest.
SATA `SSD` — flash over the SATA interface, much faster than HDD.
`NVMe` SSD — flash over PCIe in an M.2 slot, the fastest mainstream option.

sections.heading: RAID basics
sections.body: RAID combines drives for speed or redundancy: `RAID 0` stripes for speed with no protection, `RAID 1` mirrors for redundancy, `RAID 5` stripes with parity (needs 3+ disks), and `RAID 10` combines mirroring and striping.

image.alt: Comparison of RAID 0 striping, RAID 1 mirroring, and RAID 5 striping with parity across disks.
image.caption: RAID 0 = speed, RAID 1 = redundancy, RAID 5 = both via parity.

title: Power supplies and cooling
objectives: Select power supplies and cooling solutions

sections.body: The power supply converts wall AC into the DC voltages a PC needs, and cooling carries away the heat that components produce. Both are common sources of intermittent, hard-to-diagnose faults.

sections.heading: Sizing a power supply
sections.body: A power supply unit (`PSU`) is rated in watts; choose one with enough headroom for the CPU, GPU, and drives, plus the right connectors (24-pin board, CPU, and PCIe power). An undersized PSU causes random shutdowns under load.

sections.heading: Voltages and safety
sections.body: A PC PSU supplies `+3.3V`, `+5V`, and `+12V` rails. Never open a power supply — its internal capacitors can hold a dangerous charge even when unplugged. Replace, do not repair, a failed PSU.

sections.heading: Cooling
sections.body: Heat is moved by air (heatsinks and case fans) or liquid cooling loops. A thin layer of `thermal paste` between the CPU and heatsink fills microscopic gaps so heat transfers efficiently. Overheating shows up as throttling, shutdowns, or loud fans.
Printers come in a few distinct technologies, and each has a signature maintenance routine and failure mode. Knowing the imaging process points you straight at the part to service.

sections.heading: Printer types
sections.body: The main families:
bullets: Laser — uses a drum, toner, and fused heat; fast, sharp text.
Inkjet — sprays liquid ink; cheap hardware, good color.
Thermal — heats special paper; common for receipts and labels.
Impact (dot-matrix) — pins strike a ribbon; needed for carbon-copy forms.

sections.heading: The laser imaging process
sections.body: The seven steps in order: processing, charging, exposing, developing, transferring, fusing, and cleaning. A recurring exam question asks you to place these in sequence, so commit the order to memory.

sections.heading: Maintenance and connectivity
sections.body: Laser printers need periodic maintenance kits (rollers, fuser); inkjets need head cleaning and alignment. Printers are shared over USB, the network, or print servers, and modern ones support direct printing from mobile devices.

Virtualization lets one physical computer run several isolated operating systems at once. The software that makes this possible is the hypervisor, which divides real hardware into virtual machines that each believe they own the box.

sections.heading: Type 1 vs. Type 2 hypervisors
sections.body: A `Type 1` (bare-metal) hypervisor runs directly on the hardware with no host OS — fast and used in data centers (ESXi, Hyper-V). A `Type 2` (hosted) hypervisor runs as an app on top of a normal OS (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation), which is convenient for testing on a desktop.

sections.heading: Why virtualize
sections.body: Common uses: consolidating many servers onto fewer machines, sandboxing risky software, running legacy applications, and spinning up disposable test environments. A VM's whole state is just files, so it can be snapshotted and restored quickly.

sections.heading: Requirements
sections.body: Virtualization needs CPU support (Intel VT-x or AMD-V) enabled in firmware, plenty of RAM, and enough storage for each guest. Underprovisioning any of these makes the guests sluggish.
Cloud computing rents computing resources over the internet instead of owning them. The service models differ in how much the provider manages versus how much you do.

sections.heading: The three core models
sections.body: From most to least that you manage yourself:
bullets: `IaaS` (Infrastructure as a Service) — you rent virtual machines, storage, and networks and manage the OS upward.
`PaaS` (Platform as a Service) — the provider runs the OS and runtime; you just deploy your application.
`SaaS` (Software as a Service) — you simply use finished software in a browser, like email or a CRM.

sections.heading: Why it appeals
sections.body: The cloud is elastic — you scale resources up or down on demand — and metered, so you pay for what you use. That turns large up-front hardware purchases into predictable operating costs.
Beyond what a cloud provides, you choose how it is deployed — who owns it and who can use it. The right model balances control, cost, and compliance.

sections.heading: Deployment models
sections.body: The main choices:
bullets: `Public` cloud — shared infrastructure run by a provider like AWS or Azure.
`Private` cloud — dedicated to one organization for control and compliance.
`Hybrid` cloud — a mix, keeping sensitive workloads private while bursting to public for scale.
`Community` cloud — shared by organizations with common needs.

sections.heading: Shared and measured resources
sections.body: Public clouds rely on resource pooling — many customers share a pool of hardware, with each tenant isolated from the others. Rapid elasticity and measured (metered) service mean capacity grows on demand and you are billed for actual usage.
Containers are a lighter alternative to full virtual machines. Both isolate applications, but they do it at different layers, which changes their size and speed.

image.alt: Side-by-side stacks: virtual machines each run a full guest OS, while containers share the host OS kernel.
image.caption: VMs ship a full OS each; containers share the host kernel.

sections.heading: How they differ
sections.body: A VM virtualizes hardware and runs a complete guest OS, so each one is large and slower to start. A container shares the host's OS kernel and packages only the app and its dependencies, so it is small, starts in seconds, and packs densely onto a host.

sections.heading: When to use which
sections.body: Use a VM when you need strong isolation or a different operating system. Use a container to deploy and scale many copies of an application quickly and consistently — the model behind most modern cloud services.

title: The CompTIA troubleshooting method
objectives: Apply the best practice troubleshooting methodology

sections.body: CompTIA teaches a fixed six-step troubleshooting process, and the exam expects you to know it in order. The method keeps you from jumping to conclusions and ensures you actually verify and document the fix.

image.alt: The six CompTIA troubleshooting steps connected in order from Identify the problem through Document findings.
image.caption: Work the six steps in order, every time.

sections.heading: The six steps
sections.body: In sequence:
bullets: 1. Identify the problem (question the user, check for changes).
2. Establish a theory of probable cause (question the obvious).
3. Test the theory to determine the cause.
4. Establish a plan of action and implement the solution.
5. Verify full system functionality and apply preventive measures.
6. Document findings, actions, and outcomes.

sections.heading: Key habits
sections.body: Always back up data before making changes, change one thing at a time so you know what fixed it, and consider corporate policies and the user's impact. If your first theory fails, establish a new one or escalate.
Hardware faults announce themselves with characteristic symptoms. Learning to map a symptom to its likely component turns vague complaints into a focused repair.

sections.heading: Boot and power symptoms
sections.body: No power at all points to the PSU, cabling, or wall outlet. POST beep codes or no display suggest RAM, GPU, or CPU seating. A `time/date reset` on every boot is the classic dead CMOS battery.

sections.heading: Storage and memory warnings
sections.body: Failing drives give early warning: clicking or grinding noises, S.M.A.R.T. errors, very slow performance, or files that disappear. Bad RAM tends to cause random crashes and blue screens that move around rather than repeat on one app.

sections.heading: Heat and display
sections.body: Overheating causes throttling, sudden shutdowns, and loud fans — check airflow, dust, and thermal paste. Display artifacts, distortion, or a blank screen with the system running point to the GPU, cable, or the monitor itself.
When a device cannot reach the network, work outward from the host: confirm the physical link, then the IP configuration, then name resolution, then the path to the destination.

sections.heading: Read the IP address
sections.body: Run `ipconfig` (Windows) or `ifconfig`/`ip` (Linux). An address starting `169.254.x.x` is APIPA — the device failed to get a DHCP lease, so suspect the DHCP server or the link. No address at all usually means a physical or driver problem.

sections.heading: Test reachability
sections.body: Use `ping` to test connectivity to the gateway, then a public IP, then a domain name. If the IP pings but the name does not, the problem is DNS. `tracert` shows where along the path packets stop.

sections.heading: Common causes
sections.body: Typical culprits are a bad cable or port, wrong Wi-Fi password, weak signal, duplicate IP addresses, or an exhausted DHCP scope. Swapping a known-good cable is a fast way to rule out the physical layer.
Print problems split into two groups: the job will not come out at all, or it prints but looks wrong. The output defect usually identifies the failing part.

sections.heading: Jobs that will not print
sections.body: Check the obvious first: power, paper, and a cleared error. Then look at the print queue — a stuck job blocks everything behind it; clearing the queue or restarting the print spooler service often fixes it. Confirm the right printer and connection are selected.

sections.heading: Output quality on laser printers
sections.body: Defects point to parts:
bullets: Faded print — low toner or a failing transfer roller.
Repeating marks down the page — a damaged drum.
Toner smears off the page — a failed fuser (not bonding the toner).
Ghost images — drum or erase-lamp issues.

sections.heading: Paper handling
sections.body: Frequent jams come from worn pickup rollers, the wrong paper weight, humidity, or overfilled trays. Always pull jammed paper in the direction of travel to avoid leaving fragments behind.
Windows ships in several editions aimed at home, business, and enterprise users. The differences are mostly about management and security features, not the core experience.

sections.heading: The editions
sections.body: From least to most capable:
bullets: `Home` — consumer edition; lacks domain join and BitLocker.
`Pro` — adds domain join, Group Policy, BitLocker, and Remote Desktop host.
`Pro for Workstations` / `Enterprise` — higher hardware limits and advanced management.

sections.heading: Domains vs. workgroups
sections.body: A `workgroup` is a peer-to-peer group where each PC manages its own accounts — fine for homes and tiny offices. A `domain` centralizes accounts and policy on a server (Active Directory), which is why business networks require Pro or higher to join one.

sections.heading: 32-bit vs. 64-bit
sections.body: A 64-bit version of Windows can address far more than 4 GB of RAM and runs both 64-bit and 32-bit apps; a 32-bit OS is capped near 4 GB. Modern installs are 64-bit, which also requires a 64-bit CPU.

title: Windows tools and the command line
objectives: Use Windows management tools and commands

sections.body: Windows bundles a deep toolbox of utilities and commands. Knowing which one solves which problem is faster than clicking through menus, and several appear on the exam by name.

sections.heading: Management utilities
sections.body: Frequently used consoles:
bullets: `Task Manager` — processes, performance, and startup apps.
`Device Manager` — hardware and driver problems.
`Disk Management` — partitions, formatting, and drive letters.
`Event Viewer` — system and application logs for troubleshooting.

sections.heading: Essential commands
sections.body: Command-line staples: `ipconfig` for network settings, `ping`/`tracert` for connectivity, `sfc /scannow` to repair system files, `chkdsk` to check a disk, `gpupdate` to refresh policy, and `sfc`/`DISM` together to fix a corrupt image.

sections.heading: Files and navigation
sections.body: `cd` changes directory, `dir` lists files, `md`/`rd` make and remove directories, and `copy`/`xcopy`/`robocopy` move files — `robocopy` being the robust choice for large or mirrored transfers.

title: Linux and macOS basics
objectives: Identify features of other operating systems

sections.body: A+ technicians support more than Windows. You need a working familiarity with macOS features and core Linux commands without being an expert in either.

sections.heading: macOS features
sections.body: Know these by name: `Time Machine` for backups, `Spotlight` for search, `Mission Control` for window management, `Keychain` for stored credentials, `Disk Utility` for drives, and the `App Store` for software. macOS uses .dmg and .pkg installers.

sections.heading: Core Linux commands
sections.body: Common shell commands:
bullets: `ls`, `cd`, `pwd` — list and navigate.
`cp`, `mv`, `rm`, `mkdir` — manage files.
`chmod`/`chown` — change permissions and ownership.
`sudo`, `apt`/`yum`, `grep`, `ps` — elevate, install, search, list processes.

sections.heading: Filesystems
sections.body: Windows uses NTFS (and exFAT for large removable media); macOS uses APFS; Linux commonly uses ext4. Cross-platform USB drives are often formatted exFAT so every OS can read and write them.
Installing an OS is more than clicking Next. The boot method, partition scheme, and install type all affect whether data survives and whether the system boots reliably.

sections.heading: Partition styles and boot
sections.body: Modern systems use `GPT` partitioning with `UEFI` firmware, which supports drives over 2 TB and Secure Boot. Older `MBR`/BIOS is limited to 2 TB and four primary partitions. Match the firmware mode to the partition style.

image.alt: Comparison of MBR with legacy BIOS versus GPT with UEFI, listing partition and capacity limits.
image.caption: GPT/UEFI supports larger drives, more partitions, and Secure Boot.

sections.heading: Install types
sections.body: An `in-place upgrade` keeps apps and files; a `clean install` wipes the drive for a fresh start; a `repair` install fixes system files without removing data. For widespread rollouts, `image deployment` clones a prepared image to many machines over the network.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Always back up user data first, verify hardware meets requirements, and have drivers ready. After install, run updates, reinstall applications, and restore the user's data and settings.
Most breaches start by tricking a person or exploiting a weak configuration, not by breaking encryption. Recognizing the named attacks lets you spot them and teach users to resist them.

sections.heading: Social engineering
sections.body: Attacks that target people:
bullets: `Phishing` — fraudulent emails luring users to fake sites; `spear phishing` targets a specific person.
`Vishing` (voice) and `smishing` (SMS) — the same trick over phone and text.
`Tailgating` — following someone through a secure door.
`Shoulder surfing` and `dumpster diving` — stealing info by watching or digging.

sections.heading: Technical attacks
sections.body: Know `DoS`/`DDoS` (flooding a service offline), `on-path` (formerly man-in-the-middle) interception, `brute force` and `dictionary` password guessing, `SQL injection`, and `zero-day` exploits that target unknown vulnerabilities.

sections.heading: Spoofing and rogue devices
sections.body: Attackers impersonate trusted things: spoofed email addresses, an `evil twin` rogue access point mimicking a real Wi-Fi name, or DNS poisoning that sends users to malicious sites. Healthy suspicion is the best first defense.
Access control answers two questions: are you who you claim to be (authentication), and what are you allowed to do (authorization). Strong identity is the foundation of every other control.

sections.heading: Multifactor authentication
sections.body: Combine factors from different categories: something you `know` (password/PIN), something you `have` (phone, token, smart card), and something you `are` (fingerprint, face). Requiring two or more makes a stolen password far less useful.

image.alt: Three MFA factor categories — something you know, have, and are — each with examples.
image.caption: MFA combines two or more different factor categories.

sections.heading: Least privilege
sections.body: Give each user and account the minimum rights needed for the job, and no more. Use standard (non-admin) accounts for daily work, separate administrator accounts for changes, and remove access promptly when roles change.

sections.heading: Identity systems
sections.body: On Windows domains, `Active Directory` centralizes accounts and applies `Group Policy`. Single sign-on lets one login reach many services, while strong, unique passwords (ideally in a password manager) protect each identity.

title: Hardening devices and networks
objectives: Apply security best practices to devices

sections.body: Hardening means reducing the attack surface — turning off what you do not need and protecting what you do. A few consistent habits block the majority of opportunistic attacks.

sections.heading: Workstation hardening
sections.body: Core steps: keep the OS and apps patched, run reputable anti-malware, enable the host firewall, require strong passwords with screen lock and timeout, and encrypt the drive with `BitLocker` (Windows) or `FileVault` (macOS) so a lost laptop does not leak data.

sections.heading: Network hardening
sections.body: On the network, change default credentials, use `WPA2`/`WPA3` wireless encryption, disable unused ports and services, segment guest traffic, and keep firmware current. A firewall filters traffic by rules at the perimeter.

sections.heading: Mobile and physical security
sections.body: Mobile devices need screen locks, remote wipe, and app vetting. Physical controls matter too: locked server rooms, cable locks, badge access, and shredding sensitive documents close gaps that software cannot.
Malware comes in distinct types, and CompTIA defines a specific removal procedure. Knowing both the categories and the ordered steps is heavily tested.

sections.heading: Types of malware
sections.body: The main categories:
bullets: `Virus` — attaches to files and spreads when run; a `worm` spreads on its own.
`Trojan` — disguised as legitimate software.
`Ransomware` — encrypts data and demands payment.
`Spyware`/`keylogger` — secretly captures activity; a `rootkit` hides deep in the system.

sections.heading: The removal procedure
sections.body: CompTIA's order: 1) investigate and verify the symptoms, 2) quarantine the infected system, 3) disable System Restore (Windows), 4) remediate — update anti-malware and scan, 5) schedule scans and run updates, 6) re-enable System Restore and create a clean restore point, 7) educate the end user.

sections.heading: Recognizing infection
sections.body: Warning signs include pop-ups, redirected searches, sudden slowness, disabled security tools, unexpected network traffic, and files you cannot open. When malware blocks tools, boot into Safe Mode or use a bootable rescue scanner.
Windows issues range from slow boots to a system that will not start at all. A handful of recovery tools and a calm, layered approach resolve most of them.

sections.heading: Boot failures
sections.body: If Windows will not start, use the Windows Recovery Environment: `Startup Repair` fixes boot files automatically, `System Restore` rolls back recent changes, and `Safe Mode` loads minimal drivers so you can undo a bad update or driver. A 'no boot device' error points at drive or boot-order problems.

image.alt: Windows recovery options ordered from least to most drastic: Startup Repair, System Restore, Safe Mode, then Reset.
image.caption: Start with the least drastic recovery option.

sections.heading: Slow or unstable systems
sections.body: For sluggish performance, check Task Manager for resource hogs, disable unneeded startup apps, free disk space, and scan for malware. Repeated blue screens (BSOD) often trace to a bad driver, failing RAM, or overheating — note the stop code to narrow it down.

sections.heading: Repair commands
sections.body: When system files are corrupt, run `sfc /scannow` to repair them and `DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth` to fix the underlying image. `chkdsk` checks the disk for errors. Reboot and re-test after each repair.
When a single application misbehaves while the rest of the system is fine, the problem is usually local to that app — its files, settings, permissions, or compatibility.

sections.heading: Crashing or won't open
sections.body: Start simple: close and reopen, then reboot. If it persists, run as administrator to rule out permissions, check for updates, and repair or reinstall the application. Event Viewer often logs the exact faulting module.

sections.heading: Compatibility and resources
sections.body: Older programs may need Compatibility Mode or a specific runtime (such as .NET or a Visual C++ redistributable). An app that is slow or freezes may be starved of RAM or fighting another process — Task Manager shows what is consuming resources.

sections.heading: Updates gone wrong
sections.body: A bad patch can break a working app. Rolling back the update, clearing the app's cache, or restoring a previous version frequently resolves regressions introduced by an update.

title: Mobile OS and app issues
objectives: Troubleshoot mobile OS and application issues

sections.body: Phones and tablets have their own failure patterns centered on battery, connectivity, and apps. Many fixes are simple, but a few symptoms hint at security problems.

sections.heading: Battery and performance
sections.body: Fast battery drain, overheating, and slowness often come from a misbehaving app, an aging battery, or too many background processes. Close or update the offending app, check battery-usage stats, and restart the device before anything drastic.

sections.heading: Connectivity and apps
sections.body: For Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, or cellular trouble, toggle the radio or Airplane mode, forget and re-add the network, and confirm signal. Apps that crash or will not update usually clear up after updating the app, clearing its cache, or reinstalling it.

sections.heading: Security symptoms
sections.body: Watch for signs of compromise: unexpected data usage, unfamiliar apps, excessive pop-ups, or rapid battery loss. Remove unknown apps, check app permissions, and as a last resort back up and factory-reset the device.
Some 'software' problems are really security incidents. Learning to recognize the symptoms — and respond in the right order — prevents a minor infection from becoming a breach.

sections.heading: Red-flag symptoms
sections.body: Be suspicious of: redirected browser searches, a flood of pop-ups, new toolbars or home pages, disabled antivirus, certificate warnings, and slow performance with high network activity. Renamed or encrypted files signal ransomware.

sections.heading: Responding safely
sections.body: Disconnect the device from the network to stop spread, then follow the malware-removal steps: quarantine, scan and remediate, and educate the user. Do not pay ransomware demands — restore from a clean backup instead.

title: Documentation and change management
objectives: Use documentation and change management best practices

sections.body: Good IT runs on paperwork: records that let the next technician understand the environment, and a process that keeps changes from causing outages. These soft skills are tested as heavily as the hardware.

sections.heading: Documentation you should keep
sections.body: Common documents:
bullets: Network topology diagrams and asset inventories.
Knowledge base / FAQ articles for recurring issues.
`Acceptable use policy` (AUP) governing how systems may be used.
Incident reports and a ticketing record of work done.

sections.heading: Change management
sections.body: Significant changes go through a process: document the purpose and scope, assess risk, get approval from a change board, and prepare a `rollback plan` in case it goes wrong. Planned changes happen in a maintenance window, with end users notified in advance.
Protecting people and equipment is a professional baseline. That means guarding components against static, handling hazards correctly, and disposing of materials responsibly.

sections.heading: Protecting equipment from static
sections.body: Electrostatic discharge (`ESD`) can silently destroy components. Use an anti-static wrist strap connected to ground, anti-static mats and bags, and keep humidity moderate. Counter-intuitively, do not wear a wrist strap when working inside a CRT or power supply, where stored voltage is the bigger danger.

sections.heading: Personal safety
sections.body: Unplug equipment before servicing, remove jewelry, and never open power supplies, CRTs, or laser printer fusers carelessly — they hold dangerous charge or heat. Lift with your legs, and follow lockout procedures around mains power.

sections.heading: Handling and disposal
sections.body: An `SDS` (Safety Data Sheet) explains safe handling and disposal of chemicals, batteries, and toner. Batteries, CRTs, and toner are regulated e-waste — recycle them through proper channels rather than the trash. Keep equipment in stable temperature and humidity.
Technical skill earns trust only when paired with professionalism. How you communicate and handle sensitive situations often matters more to the user than the fix itself.

sections.heading: Communicating with users
sections.body: Use clear language and avoid jargon, listen actively without interrupting, and set and meet expectations about timing. Be culturally sensitive, stay positive, and confirm the issue is resolved to the user's satisfaction before closing the ticket.

sections.heading: Professional conduct
sections.body: Be on time, dress appropriately, and avoid distractions like personal calls during a job. Maintain confidentiality of materials on a user's device, and deal with difficult customers by staying calm, not arguing, and not taking it personally.

sections.heading: Handling sensitive data
sections.body: If you encounter prohibited content or a security incident, follow the chain of custody and your organization's reporting procedure — document everything and preserve evidence rather than acting on your own.
Backups are the safety net behind every other procedure. Knowing the backup types and following a tested strategy is what turns a disaster into a minor inconvenience.

sections.heading: Backup types
sections.body: How much each one copies:
bullets: `Full` — everything; slowest to back up, fastest to restore.
`Incremental` — only what changed since the last backup of any kind; fast backup, slower restore.
`Differential` — everything changed since the last full backup; a middle ground.
`Synthetic full` — a full assembled from previous backups without re-reading all source data.

sections.heading: The 3-2-1 rule
sections.body: Keep `3` copies of important data on `2` different media with `1` copy stored off-site (or in the cloud). Off-site protects against fire, theft, and ransomware that would otherwise take the originals and local backups together.

image.alt: The 3-2-1 backup rule shown as three numbered cards: 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 off-site.
image.caption: 3 copies, on 2 media, with 1 off-site.

sections.heading: Test your restores
sections.body: A backup you have never restored is only a hope. Periodically perform a test restore to confirm the data is intact and recoverable, and document the recovery steps so anyone can follow them under pressure.
